policy_statement: Ensure 100% of roadworks in priority intervention zones and upstream of flood-prone areas incorporate Integrated Stormwater Management (ISWM).
mechanism_description: The City Council commits to incorporating integrated stormwater (rainwater) management into roadworks in priority areas to reduce flooding risk in flood-prone zones.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard (heavy rainfall) → improved stormwater capture/infiltration/detention → ↓ flood extent/peak flows → ↓ exposure & damages
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Changed system/activity: Road reconstruction and streetscape projects are designed and built to include integrated stormwater measures (e.g., permeable surfaces, bioswales, infiltration trenches, curb cuts, detention/retention, upgraded inlets and conveyance) in priority/upstream areas.  
(2) Mediating process: These measures increase infiltration and temporary storage and/or improve drainage capacity, reducing runoff volume and delaying/attenuating peak discharge to the sewer/storm network and surface flow paths.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower frequency and severity of pluvial flooding (and associated service disruption and property damage), increasing resilience to more intense rainfall consistent with climate change.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reducing urban flood damages by managing runoff during intense rainfall events.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The mechanism acts on runoff management to reduce flooding impacts from heavy rainfall (a climate hazard), i.e., hazard → reduced exposure/damage.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: Any mitigation co-benefits (e.g., minor energy savings in pumping or avoided repair emissions) are secondary; the intended and largest effect is risk reduction from flooding. Resource efficiency is not the core outcome (it’s not primarily about reducing water/energy inputs for the same service), and nature-based solutions may be used but are not required by the mechanism (ISWM can be green or grey).  
3) Edge cases applied: If implemented specifically via green infrastructure, it could overlap with Nature-Based Solutions, but the dominant effect remains flood-risk reduction (adaptation) regardless of asset type.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy matching “integrated stormwater management in roadworks → urban flood risk reduction” fundamentally targets pluvial flood resilience through drainage/runoff control, so Adaptation is consistently primary.  
5) Note on secondary subtypes: Any secondary tagging (e.g., NBS vs grey infrastructure, combined sewer overflow reduction, water quality co-benefits) must be determined at the individual policy level based on explicit design/implementation details.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If the policy explicitly mandates vegetated systems for co-benefits like urban cooling and carbon sequestration, an NBS secondary label may be appropriate; if framed mainly as sewer overflow pollution control rather than flood risk, the primary could shift in a different mechanism definition, but under the stated mechanism flood-risk reduction dominates.
key_indicators: “Generalise Integrated Stormwater Management (ISWM) during public works”; “committed to ensuring that 100% of roadworks”; “priority intervention zones”; “upstream of flood-prone areas”; “incorporate integrated rainwater management”
co_benefits: Improved water quality (reduced polluted runoff), urban heat mitigation/greening and amenity, groundwater recharge, potential biodiversity benefits.
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be framed as a procurement/design standard for municipal capital works, but the “ensure 100%… incorporate” language reads as a mandate/requirement rather than a voluntary program or mere target.
